name: Clarent
description: Clarent was a sword belonging to King Arthur in Arthurian legend. It is often described as a sword of peace, used for knighting ceremonies and other formal occasions, rather than for battle. Its origins are somewhat vague, but it is generally understood to be a royal sword, signifying Arthur's kingship and authority. In some versions of the legend, Clarent is described as being kept in a chest within Arthur's armory, reserved for peaceful purposes. However, its most infamous appearance occurs in the story of Mordred's betrayal. Mordred, Arthur's illegitimate son (or nephew, depending on the version), seized the throne of Camelot while Arthur was away fighting in Gaul. In his usurpation, Mordred stole Clarent from Arthur's armory. Because it was a sword not meant for battle, some accounts suggest it was easier to wield or manipulate by someone not skilled in combat. Mordred used Clarent to strike the first blow against Arthur's loyalists, initiating the final, devastating battle that ultimately led to Arthur's downfall. The act of using Clarent for violence, especially considering its peaceful purpose, is seen as a significant violation and a symbol of Mordred's treachery and lack of legitimacy. The sword's association with betrayal and the demise of Camelot makes it a potent symbol of lost innocence and the tragic consequences of internal conflict. The use of a peaceful object for such violence highlights the corruption and moral decay that ultimately consumed Arthur's kingdom.
subtitle: The Sword of Peace, Turned to War
origin: Royal sword, likely crafted by skilled artisans for a king.
currentLocation: Unknown, presumably lost after the Battle of Camlann.

keyMyths.title: Mordred's Treachery
keyMyths.description: Mordred stole Clarent and used it to initiate the final battle, symbolizing his betrayal and the corruption of Arthur's kingdom.
keyMyths.source: Le Morte d'Arthur by Thomas Malory

keyMyths.title: Sword of Peace
keyMyths.description: Clarent was intended for ceremonial purposes, highlighting the tragedy of its use in violence and the loss of innocence.
keyMyths.source: Geoffrey of Monmouth's Historia Regum Britanniae

sources.author: Thomas Malory
sources.description: A comprehensive compilation of Arthurian legends.
sources.text: A comprehensive compilation of Arthurian legends.

sources.author: Geoffrey of Monmouth
